title: Welfare professionals n.e.c.
description: Counsel and advise individuals with alcohol, tobacco, drug, or other problems, such as gambling and eating disorders. May counsel individuals, families, or groups or engage in prevention programs. Diagnose and treat mental and emotional disorders, whether cognitive, affective, or behavioral, within the context of marriage and family systems. Apply psychotherapeutic and family systems theories and techniques in the delivery of services to individuals, couples, and families for the purpose of treating such diagnosed nervous and mental disorders. Counsel and advise individuals and groups to promote optimum mental and emotional health, with an emphasis on prevention. May help individuals deal with a broad range of mental health issues, such as those associated with addictions and substance abuse; family, parenting, and marital problems; stress management; self-esteem; or aging. (Based on 18 related US occupations.)

tasks.task: Counsel clients or patients, individually or in group sessions, to assist in overcoming dependencies, adjusting to life, or making changes.
Assess clients' cognitive abilities and physical and emotional needs to determine appropriate interventions.
Develop client treatment plans based on research, clinical experience, and client histories.
Monitor, evaluate, and record client progress according to measurable goals described in treatment and care plan.
Develop and maintain relationships with community referral sources, such as schools or community groups.
Collaborate with mental health professionals and other staff members to perform clinical assessments or develop treatment plans.
Provide clients or family members with information about addiction issues and available services or programs, making appropriate referrals when necessary.
Conduct crisis interventions to ensure the safety of the patients and others involved.
Educate clients or community members about mental or physical illness, abuse, medication, or available community resources.
Collaborate with community agencies to establish facilities and programs for persons with disabilities.
Collaborate with counselors, physicians, or nurses to plan or coordinate treatment, drawing on social work experience and patient needs.
Counsel clients or patients, individually or in group sessions, to assist in overcoming dependencies and making beneficial changes.
Monitor, evaluate, and record client progress with respect to treatment goals.
Refer patients, clients, or families to community resources for housing or treatment, ensuring follow-through to ensure service efficacy.
Counsel or aid family members to support clients or patients in understanding and dealing with their issues.
Modify treatment plans according to changes in client status and treatment needs.
Plan or conduct programs to prevent substance abuse or improve community health services.
Maintain confidentiality of records relating to clients' treatment, gathering information through interviews, observations, or tests.
Develop and implement treatment plans based on client needs and assess the effectiveness of counseling programs on client progress.
Advocate for clients or patients to resolve crises and support their rehabilitation and social integration.
Gather information about community mental health needs or resources that could be used in conjunction with therapy.
Maintain case history records and prepare reports to document progress and necessary adjustments in treatment.
Evaluate physical or mental conditions based on client information and supervise other workers providing services.
Investigate child abuse or neglect cases, take authorized protective actions, and address legal issues as necessary.
Conduct social research and develop or advise on social policy to assist in community development.
Lead group counseling sessions that provide support in areas such as grief, stress, or chemical dependency.
Prepare and maintain case folders for clients and ensure accurate documentation of services provided.
Discuss with clients their plans for life after therapy and educate them about available resources.
